executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

**Enrollment and Spending Trends**

National Medicaid enrollment stands at 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with nearly all major states experiencing enrollment declines following the end of pandemic-era continuous coverage protections. Most significantly, Michigan reported a 7.6% year-over-year decrease, while California and Illinois both declined 6.2%. Texas, with 4.0 million enrollees, experienced a more moderate 3.1% decline, matching Pennsylvania's rate and outperforming Florida's 4.0% decrease. Only North Carolina bucked the national trend with a slight 0.5% enrollment increase. Total national Medicaid expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a national per-enrollee average of $2,869. Texas spending totaled $12.0 billion with a per-enrollee cost of $2,988, positioning the state slightly above the national average but significantly below high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,377 per enrollee) and Massachusetts ($4,201 per enrollee).

**Quality Performance and State Comparisons**

Quality measure performance reveals substantial interstate variation, with national scores averaging 65.6 out of 100 points. Ten states achieved high-performer status (75+ points), led by Massachusetts (81), Minnesota (80), and Vermont (79), while fifteen states scored below 60 points, with Mississippi ranking lowest at 48 points. Texas scored 62 points, ranking 30th nationally in the "moderate" performance tier, indicating room for improvement compared to leading states but avoiding the bottom quartile. The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% significantly exceeds the national average of 83.2%, suggesting strong care coordination infrastructure that could support quality improvement initiatives. Recent federal regulatory activity includes proposed 2028 HCBS quality measures and updated data collection requirements, signaling continued federal emphasis on accountability and performance measurement across state programs.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the third-largest Medicaid program nationally with 4.03 million enrollees, trailing only California and New York. Despite its size, Texas demonstrates notable cost efficiency with per-enrollee spending of $2,988—4.2% above the national average of $2,869 but significantly below expansion states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Ohio ($3,561). This moderate spending reflects Texas's strategic focus on managed care delivery, achieving 93.3% penetration compared to the 83.2% national average. The state's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, closely matching the national trend as states complete post-pandemic eligibility redeterminations. Among southern peer states, Texas's spending pattern aligns with North Carolina ($2,616) and exceeds Florida ($2,031), while its enrollment trajectory mirrors similar non-expansion states experiencing PHE unwinding effects.

However, Texas faces significant quality challenges that warrant attention. With a quality score of 62/100, Texas ranks 30th nationally and falls into the "moderate" performance tier—concerning for a state of its size and resources. This performance notably lags behind expansion peers and even trails some southern states like North Carolina. The state's quality metrics place it uncomfortably close to the bottom-performing southern states (Arkansas at 54, South Carolina at 53), suggesting systemic care delivery issues that the high managed care penetration hasn't resolved. While Texas's cost efficiency and managed care infrastructure represent programmatic strengths, the quality performance gap—15 points below high-performing states—indicates urgent need for targeted quality improvement initiatives, particularly given the state's substantial non-expansion coverage limitations and the vulnerable populations served within current eligibility constraints.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost efficiency issues.

alerts.title: Florida Exceptionally Low Per-Enrollee Spending
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, potentially indicating access or adequacy concerns.

alerts.title: Quality Performance Crisis in Southern States
alerts.description: Five states (MS, OK, AL, SC, AR) show critically low quality scores below 55, with Mississippi at only 48/100.

alerts.title: Northeast States Show Excellence in Quality Metrics
alerts.description: Massachusetts, Vermont, and Rhode Island achieve quality scores above 78, demonstrating best practices for replication.

alerts.title: High-Cost, Moderate-Quality Pattern in Northeast
alerts.description: Pennsylvania combines exceptionally high spending ($4,377/enrollee) with quality performance not reflected in expenditure levels.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score at risk tier signals potential CMS intervention
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid quality score of 62/100 ranks #30 nationally, placing it in moderate tier but only 2 points above the low performer threshold of <60. With 15 states already classified as low performers, Texas is vulnerable to quality-based penalties or enhanced oversight.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality scores directly impact federal funding and CMS oversight intensity. A drop below 60 could trigger enhanced federal scrutiny, affecting contract performance metrics and renewal prospects.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS quality measures for 2028 will impact Texas operations
executiveInsights.summary: CMS announced new 2028 Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set through Federal Register filing. Given Texas's current moderate quality performance, new HCBS requirements will likely require operational adjustments.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: HCBS represents a growing Medicaid focus area, and new quality measures will require system updates, reporting changes, and potentially new vendor capabilities to maintain compliance.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review Federal Register filing for HCBS quality requirements impact assessment

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs 4.2% above national average despite efficiency gains
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee compared to national average of $2,868, representing $120 premium per member. With 4.03M enrollees, this translates to approximately $484M in above-average spending annually.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-member costs amid enrollment decline suggests either increased acuity or operational inefficiencies that could pressure state budget and contract economics. This creates opportunity for cost optimization initiatives.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Analyze Texas cost drivers versus comparable states for optimization opportunities

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment decline of 3.1% signals unwinding stabilization opportunity
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment decreased 3.1% YoY to 4.03M members, which is less severe than several large states including California (-6.2%) and Michigan (-7.6%). Only North Carolina among major states shows growth (+0.5%).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Moderate enrollment decline suggests Texas is managing post-PHE unwinding better than peer states, creating stability for contract performance and potential model for other state engagements.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Document Texas unwinding best practices for replication in other state contracts

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration 10.1 points above national average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration versus 83.2% national average, with 3.86M of 4.03M enrollees in managed care. This represents one of the highest managed care adoption rates nationally.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration indicates sophisticated state operations and strong MCO relationships, positioning Texas as a reference state for managed care optimization strategies across Accenture's Medicaid portfolio.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Leverage Texas managed care model for proposals in states with lower penetration

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating performance improvement pressure and potential penalties.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement roadmap targeting top-performing state benchmarks.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline trend
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY with 4M+ enrollees, indicating unwinding impacts and funding pressures.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Model enrollment scenarios and develop cost optimization strategies for reduced member base.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality expansion
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Home and Community-Based Services quality measures create compliance requirements and service delivery changes.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position Accenture as HCBS quality measurement implementation partner ahead of 2028 requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: High 93.3% managed care penetration in Texas suggests upcoming MCO contract renewals worth billions in administration.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor HHSC procurement portal actively and prepare MCO administrative services proposals.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio oversight creates compliance risks but also remediation consulting opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring capabilities and develop OIG remediation response frameworks.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas $2,988 per-enrollee cost exceeds national average, creating pressure for administrative cost reduction initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop cost reduction proposals targeting administrative efficiency and technology modernization.

riskOpportunity.label: Data collection modernization
riskOpportunity.description: New CHIP generic information collection activities signal upcoming data infrastructure and reporting system changes.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Prepare data modernization and analytics solutions for enhanced Medicaid reporting requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive procurement landscape
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple state procurement portals indicate active RFP environment creating both opportunity capture and competitive threats.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish systematic procurement intelligence across Texas ESBD and competitor state activities.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid serves 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee annually in fiscal year 2024.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees in managed care.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a moderate quality tier score of 62 out of 100.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active procurement portals identified for Texas HHSC opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Six active OIG and regulatory items detected in Federal Register and HHS oversight portfolio.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific milestone date available in current data.
